title: Colouring paper pulp with natural pigments
subtitle: How to produce consistent coloured sheets using earth and plant pigments
excerpt: Natural earth pigments, ochre, raw sienna, umber, lampblack, mix into paper pulp to produce muted, stable colours without the risk of synthetic dye migration. The colours are lightfast and non-fugitive. Mixing is straightforward; the challenge is consistency across a batch.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923): Hunter documents the use of natural earths and carbon black in Asian and early European paper traditions. The specific technique of adding earth pigments to Western pulp is consistent with workshop practice documented in Hunter's source materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: A naturally occurring mineral pigment, typically an iron oxide or manganese compound, used for colouring. Ochre, sienna, umber, and red oxide are all earth pigments.
A colour that fades under light exposure. Many plant-based dyes are fugitive; mineral pigments are not.

content.text: Adding pigment to pulp produces colour that runs through the full thickness of the sheet, not just on the surface. Unlike surface dyeing or toning (applying tea or coffee to the finished sheet), pulp-coloured paper won't fade at the cut edge or produce a different colour on each face.
are the safest choice: stable, non-toxic in the quantities used for papermaking, and inherently

items.name: Cotton linter (half-stuff sheets)
items.notes: The base fibre. Cotton linter takes pigment evenly and produces a clean, bright colour.

items.name: Earth pigment powder
items.notes: Yellow ochre, raw sienna, raw umber, lampblack, or red iron oxide. Available from art suppliers in small quantities. Start with 2-4% pigment by dry fibre weight.

items.name: Paper mould + deckle, A5 or A4
items.notes: A5 for small test batches before committing to A4.

items.name: Measuring spoons and small scale
items.notes: Consistency across the batch requires weighing the pigment each time.

content.text: Mix the measured pigment powder with a small amount of water to a smooth slurry, roughly the consistency of thin cream, before adding it to the pulp. Earth pigments do not dissolve in water; they need physical dispersion. A poorly dispersed pigment produces a streaky sheet with concentrated pigment clumps.

Pour the pigment slurry into the prepared pulp vat and stir thoroughly for at least two minutes. Pull a test sheet immediately and allow it to dry before committing the rest of the batch. The wet colour will appear darker than the dry sheet, allow for this when judging the shade.

Pigment settles as the vat is used. Stir the vat thoroughly before every pull. If a batch of sheets shows colour variation from first to last, the stirring between pulls was insufficient. Some papermakers add a small amount of formation aid (polyethylene oxide) to keep the pigment more evenly suspended, a few drops per litre of vat water is enough.
